title: Find Relief from a Queasy Stomach with These 9 Herbal Tinctures

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Gingerol is good for a queasy stomach because it helps to soothe and calm the digestive system by reducing inflammation and easing nausea.

Shogaol is good for queasy stomach because it has been found to reduce inflammation and slow down the movement of the digestive system, allowing the stomach to settle and preventing nausea and vomiting.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale tincture, also known as ginger tincture, is good for a queasy stomach because it helps to calm the digestive system. The main compound in ginger, called gingerol, has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce nausea and vomiting. When you take ginger tincture, the active ingredients are quickly absorbed into the bloodstream, which helps to calm the stomach and intestines. This can be especially helpful during times of motion sickness, such as when traveling by car or boat. Ginger tincture can also be used to alleviate symptoms of morning sickness during pregnancy. It works by relaxing the muscles in the stomach and reducing the production of stomach acid, which can help to soothe a queasy stomach and prevent vomiting.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make an Angelica archangelica tincture that helps with a queasy stomach, start by preparing the herb. First, you'll need to dry the Angelica root and leaves completely, either by air-drying them or using a food dehydrator. Once dry, chop the herb into small pieces and place it in a clean glass jar. Next, pour a high-proof spirit like vodka or glycerin over the herb, making sure that the herb is completely covered. The general ratio is one part herb to two or three parts spirit. Seal the jar tightly and let it sit in a cool, dark place for two to six weeks, shaking the jar every few days. This will allow the spirit to extract the healing properties from the herb. After the extraction time has passed, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass bottle, discarding the solids. Your Angelica archangelica tincture is now ready to use - simply take a few drops in water or under the tongue as needed to help soothe a queasy stomach.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra tincture, also known as licorice root tincture, can help with a queasy stomach. The main reason for this is its anti-inflammatory properties. The licorice root contains a compound called glycyrrhizin, which reduces swelling in the stomach lining. This can help soothe stomach irritation and discomfort caused by inflammation. Additionally, the tincture has natural anti-nausea properties, which can help alleviate queasiness and vomiting. It can also help regulate digestion and bowel movements, which can contribute to a queasy stomach. Furthermore, licorice root tincture has been traditionally used to treat stomach ulcers and heartburn, which are often linked to a queasy stomach. By reducing inflammation and promoting digestion, the licorice root tincture can provide relief from queasiness and stomach discomfort.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make an Angelica archangelica tincture to help with a queasy stomach, start by gathering a handful of fresh or dried Angelica roots. Wear gloves to protect your skin from any oils or sap on the plant. Next, chop the roots into small pieces and add them to a clean glass jar, covering them completely with a high-proof spirit like vodka or rum. The spirit will help draw out the medicinal properties of the Angelica. Seal the jar tightly and store it in a cool, dark place for about 2-3 weeks, shaking the jar every day to help the spirit and Angelica mix. After this time, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids. Transfer the tincture to smaller bottles and store them in a cool, dark place. To use the tincture, mix a few drops with water and take it as needed to help soothe a queasy stomach.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Angelica archangelica herbal tincture that helps with a queasy stomach, you will need some dried or fresh roots of the plant, a jar or container with a lid, and a solvent like vodka or glycerin. First, chop the roots into small pieces and fill a clean glass jar about one-quarter full. Next, pour enough solvent over the roots to cover them completely, making sure the liquid level is at least an inch above the roots. Secure the lid tightly and let the mixture steep in a cool, dark place for about two weeks, shaking the jar every day or two to help the herbs release their active properties. After two weeks, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass container, discarding the solids. The resulting tincture can be taken in small amounts, usually 10 to 30 drops, mixed with water or taken directly, to help soothe an upset stomach and alleviate queasiness.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make an Angelica archangelica tincture that can help with a queasy stomach, start by gathering high-quality dried roots of the plant, also known as wild celery or archangel root. remedies.remedy_desc: Angelica archangelica tincture is a herbal remedy that can help with a queasy stomach. The plant, also known as wild celery, has been used for centuries in traditional medicine. It contains compounds that help calm the digestive system and reduce inflammation. When taken as a tincture, these compounds can ease nausea and vomiting, making it easier to eat and digest food. Angelica archangelica tincture is often used to treat stomach problems like indigestion and bloating, and it can also help with motion sickness. The tincture works by relaxing the muscles in the stomach and reducing spasms, which can help to calm a queasy stomach. Taking a small amount of Angelica archangelica tincture before eating or when feeling queasy may help to alleviate symptoms and make you feel better.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Angelica archangelica herbal tincture that helps with a queasy stomach, start by gathering fresh or dried Angelica roots or rhizomes. You can find these in herbal stores or grow them yourself in a sunny, well-drained spot. Next, chop the Angelica into small pieces and combine it with a neutral carrier oil like grapeseed or sunflower oil in a clean glass jar. The general ratio is one part Angelica to three parts oil. Seal the jar and place it in a cool, dark spot for several weeks, shaking the jar every day to help the Angelica infuse into the oil. After four to six weeks, strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass bottle, discarding the solids. To make a tincture, mix the infused oil with a high-proof spirit like vodka or rum in a 1:5 ratio, for example one part oil to five parts spirit. Store the tincture in a dark glass bottle and take a few drops as needed to soothe a queasy stomach.

intro_desc: Herbal tinctures can be a great way to soothe a queasy stomach. One key herb used for this purpose is 'Zingiber officinale', also known as ginger. Ginger has natural anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce nausea and vomiting. It also has a calming effect on the digestive system, making it easier to keep food down.  Another herb used to calm the stomach is 'Glycyrrhiza glabra', also known as licorice root. Licorice root has anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe the digestive tract and reduce inflammation in the stomach. It also has a sweet, soothing effect that can help calm digestive issues.  A queasy stomach can be a real problem, making it difficult to eat and enjoy meals. This can lead to weight loss, fatigue, and a general feeling of being unwell. If left untreated, stomach issues can even lead to more serious health problems, such as digestive disorders or nutrient deficiencies.  Fixing a queasy stomach can greatly improve your life. When you're able to eat and digest food easily, you have more energy and are less likely to get sick. You can also enjoy your favorite foods without feeling uncomfortable or anxious. Taking care of your stomach health can also boost your mood and overall sense of well-being. By using herbal tinctures like ginger and licorice root, you can take the first step towards a healthier, happier you.
supplementary_best: A good combination for a queasy stomach is a blend of Zingiber officinale, Glycyrrhiza glabra, and Foeniculum vulgare. Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, is a natural anti-inflammatory that helps to calm the stomach and reduce nausea. Its active compound, gingerol, has been shown to be effective in treating stomach upset. Glycyrrhiza glabra, or licorice root, is known for its anti-inflammatory and soothing properties, which can help to calm the stomach lining and reduce inflammation. Foeniculum vulgare, or fennel, contains a compound called anethole, which has natural antispasmodic and carminative properties, helping to ease digestive discomfort and prevent gas. When combined, these three herbs can work synergistically to soothe a queasy stomach and alleviate nausea, making them an effective combination for digestive issues.
supplementary_avoid: If you have a queasy stomach, it's best to avoid herbal tinctures that can irritate or worsen digestive issues. One such herb is 'Garden Sage' (Salvia officinalis), which can cause stomach upset and heartburn due to its high concentration of thujone, a compound that can also stimulate the stomach and make digestive problems worse. 'Yarrow' (Achillea millefolium) is another herb to be cautious with, as it can cause stomach cramps, diarrhea, and nausea in some people, especially when taken in large doses. 'Dandelion root' (Taraxacum officinale) can also be problematic, as its bitter compounds can stimulate the stomach and cause digestive discomfort, especially in people with sensitive stomachs. Finally, 'Sassafras' (Sassafras albidum) tincture is not recommended for those with queasy stomachs, as it contains safrole, a compound that can cause stomach upset and nausea. These herbs may interact with medications or worsen existing health conditions, so it's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using them.

When you eat ginger, it helps slow down digestion and absorbs the stomach acid, making it easier to keep food down. Some people also take ginger in a tea form or as a capsule. Ginger has been used for thousands of years in Asian cultures to treat stomach problems and is still widely used today.  Another remedy is to drink apple cider vinegar. Apple cider vinegar has a balancing effect on the body's pH levels and helps to digest food properly. It also has probiotic properties that can help restore the good bacteria in the stomach, which is often disrupted by stomach problems. Drinking a small amount of apple cider vinegar before meals can help reduce nausea and alleviate queasy stomach symptoms.  Probiotic-rich foods, such as yogurt and kefir, can also help soothe a queasy stomach. These foods contain live cultures that can help restore the balance of good and bad bacteria in the stomach. When you eat probiotic-rich foods, they can help reduce inflammation and alleviate digestive problems. Some people also take probiotic supplements to get the benefits of probiotics.  Aloe vera juice is another remedy that can help with a queasy stomach. Aloe vera has anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe the stomach and reduce inflammation. It also has a balancing effect on the body's digestive system and can help regulate bowel movements. Drinking aloe vera juice can help alleviate nausea and reduce stomach cramps.  Finally, peppermint oil can be used to help with a queasy stomach. When you inhale peppermint oil, it can help calm the stomach and reduce nausea. It also has a soothing effect on the muscles in the stomach, which can help alleviate stomach cramps. Some people also take peppermint oil in a capsule form to get the benefits of peppermint oil.
supplementary_related_ailments: People who have a queasy stomach, also known as nausea, often experience other ailments that can be relieved with medicinal herbs. One common issue is indigestion or heartburn, which is usually caused by stomach acid flowing back up into the esophagus. The herb ginger can help alleviate this by reducing inflammation and improving digestion. Peppermint oil can also calm the stomach and reduce symptoms of indigestion.  Another related issue is constipation, which can be caused by a queasy stomach making it hard for food to pass through the digestive system. The herb psyllium, also known as ispaghula, can help soften stool and make bowel movements easier. It works by absorbing excess water in the digestive system and adding bulk to stool.  Some people with a queasy stomach may also experience diarrhea, which can be caused by an imbalance of gut bacteria. The herb slippery elm can help soothe and protect the lining of the digestive tract, reducing inflammation and discomfort. It can also help replace lost electrolytes and fluids.  People with a queasy stomach may also be more prone to acid reflux, a condition where stomach acid flows back up into the esophagus, causing a burning sensation. The herb licorice root can help soothe and protect the esophagus, reducing inflammation and discomfort.  Finally, people with a queasy stomach may experience fatigue and dizziness due to a lack of nutrients being absorbed by the body. The herb ashwagandha can help boost energy and reduce stress, while also improving digestion and nutrient absorption.
